2606.11105.title: PhantomBench: Benchmarking the Non-existential Threat of Language Models
2606.11105.paper_summary: Hallucinations, where language models (LMs) generate factually ungrounded responses, pose serious risks, as users tend to blindly rely on them. This is particularly concerning in high-stakes domains, where consequences of such model behavior can lead to significant harms. Despite notable progress in understanding hallucinations, it remains unclear how reliably these models can recognize the limits of their knowledge. We introduce PhantomBench, the first large-scale benchmark of its kind, comprising more than 60K non-existent terms and entities derived from real concepts across diverse domains. Using our benchmark, we evaluate a total of 21 models of various types and sizes. We show staggering hallucination rates across the board (with average rates as high as 86.7% in some cases), and note that even frontier models surprisingly fail to abstain on non-existent concepts, especially when the input presumes their existence. We then show that PhantomBench can serve as a proxy for studying model behavior on rare concepts for which models are more prone to hallucinate. We also provide a pipeline to construct PhantomBench, enabling scalable generation of non-existent concepts tailored to the specific needs of researchers and practitioners.
2606.11105.paper_summary_zh: 幻覺，即語言模型（LMs）生成事實上無根據的回應，帶來了嚴重的風險，因為用戶往往會盲目依賴它們。這在高風險領域尤其令人擔憂，因為這種模型行為的後果可能導致重大損害。儘管在理解幻覺方面取得了顯著進展，但這些模型能多可靠地識別其知識的界限仍然不清楚。我們介紹了 PhantomBench，這是首個此類的大規模基準，包含超過 60K 個不存在的術語和實體，這些術語和實體源自於不同領域的真實概念。利用我們的基準，我們評估了總共 21 種不同類型和大小的模型。我們顯示出驚人的幻覺率（在某些情況下平均高達 86.7%），並指出即使是前沿模型在面對不存在的概念時也令人驚訝地無法避免，特別是當輸入假設它們存在時。我們接著展示了 PhantomBench 可以作為研究模型在稀有概念上的行為的代理，因為模型在這些概念上更容易產生幻覺。我們還提供了一個構建 PhantomBench 的流程，能夠根據研究人員和從業者的具體需求可擴展地生成不存在的概念。
2606.11105.author: Haeji Jung et.al.
